elements.sent-2411-001: Nonlinear filtering is a pivotal problem that has attracted significant attention from mathematicians, statisticians, engineers, and various other scientific disciplines.
elements.sent-2411-002: The solution to this problem is governed by the so-called "filtering equations." In this paper, we investigate the uniqueness of solutions to these equations within measure spaces and introduce a novel, generalized framework for this analysis.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our approach provides new insights and extends the applicability of existing theories in the study of nonlinear filtering.
elements.sent-2411-004: Keywords: Non-Linear Filtering, Measure Valued Processes, Stochastic Partial Differential Equations, Backward SPDEs, Uniqueness of Solutions.
elements.sent-2411-005: The objective of nonlinear filtering is to estimate an evolving dynamical system, modeled by a stochastic process $X$, referred to as the signal process.
elements.sent-2411-006: This signal process cannot be directly observed; instead, it is inferred through a related process $Y$, known as the observation process.
elements.sent-2411-007: The filtering problem involves determining the conditional distribution of the signal at the current time, based on the observation data accumulated up to that time.
elements.sent-2411-008: For a comprehensive treatment of the filtering problem and a historical overview of its extensive development over the past 80 years, starting with the foundational work of Kolmogorov, Krein, and Wiener, readers are encouraged to consult \cite{cr .}
elements.sent-2411-009: The conditional distribution of the signal given the observation is the solution of a nonlinear evolution equation.
elements.sent-2411-010: Moreover, it has a version which satisfies a linear evolution equations.
elements.sent-2411-011: In what follows we shall refer to these to equations as the filtering equations.
elements.sent-2411-012: The filtering equations have been studied at length by many different contributors to the filtering problem and in different frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-013: The most popular continuous time framework is that where the signal $X$ satisfies a stochastic differential equation driven by a multi-dimensional Brownian motion denoted by $V$, and the observation $Y$ satisfies an evolution equation of the form \begin{equation}dY_{t}=h(t,X_{t})\,\mathrm{d}t+d{W}_{t},\end{equation} where the driving Brownian motion $W$ is independent of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-014: In this case, the filtering equations are well understood and their solution is shown to be unique in suitably chosen spaces of measures, see, e.g., \cite{bc , for details.}
elements.sent-2411-015: In this set-up, the equations satisfied by the signal and the observation are asymmetric.
elements.sent-2411-016: There is no dependence of $X$ on $Y$, and the observational noise $W$ is chosen independent of the signal and with a coefficient that does not depend on either $X$ or $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-017: Possibly a symmetric set-up would require the pair ${\mathcal{X :=(X,Y)$ to satisfy a stochastic differential of the form \begin{equation}d{\mathcal{X}}_{t}=a(t,{\mathcal{X}}_{t})\,\mathrm{d}t+b(t,{\mathcal{X}} _{t})d{B}_{t},\end{equation} where $B:=(V,W)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, perfect symmetry is not really possible.
elements.sent-2411-019: For example, should the diffusion observation coefficient depend on the signal, the solution will degenerate, see, e.g., \cite{ckx for details.}
elements.sent-2411-020: In this paper, we introduce a framework which is as close as possible to a symmetric one, see equations (\ref{X ) and (\ref{Y ) below.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: This new framework represents a generalization of several existing ones, including those covered in \cite {bensoussan and \cite{pp2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: A simplified version of the present set-up (where the signal and the observation noises are independent) was studied in \cite{FDP .}
elements.sent-2411-023: In the new framework the coefficients of the stochastic differential equation satisfied by $X$ depend on the pair $(X,Y)$.
elements.sent-2411-024: Moreover $X$ is driven by the pair $(V,W)$ and not just by $V$.
elements.sent-2411-025: Put it differently the Brownian motion driving the signal is corellated with the Brownian motion driving the observation.
elements.sent-2411-026: Moreover we do not assume that the diffusion coefficient in the observation equation is invertible.
elements.sent-2411-027: In the degenerate case when the diffusion coefficient is equal to 0, the observation becomes independent of the signal: in this case the conditional distribution of the signal coincides with the (prior) distribution of the signal.
elements.sent-2411-028: Within this framework we obtain the filtering equations and show that they have a unique solution within a suitably chosen space of measures.
elements.sent-2411-029: The following are contributions of this paper:.
elements.sent-2411-030: We deduce the filtering equations for the signal and the observation processes satisfying equations (\ref{X )--(\ref{Y ) below.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The coefficients of both equations depend on the signal-observation pair.
elements.sent-2411-032: Moreover, as opposed to frameworks treated elsewhere we do not assume an invertible diffusion term in the observation equation.
elements.sent-2411-033: We do this at the expense of assuming a special form for the observation function $h$, see (\ref{h ) below.}
elements.sent-2411-034: We show the equivalence of the uniqueness properties of the two filtering equations, namely the nonlinear Kushner--Stratonovich equation for the conditional distribution, and the linear Zakai equation for the ``unnormalized conditional distribution'', see equations \eqref{eq:filterEqns:ks and, respectively, \eqref{eq:filterEqns:zakaifunctint below.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: We establish the uniqueness of the solution of the equation satisfied by the unnormalized conditional distribution of the signal in the space of measure valued processes.
elements.sent-2411-036: Let us now explain the novelty of our proof of the uniqueness of the Zakai equation.
elements.sent-2411-037: The so-called ``duality argument'' is a standard method for proving uniqueness of the solution of a linear equation.
elements.sent-2411-038: Bensoussan applied the duality argument in \cite{bensoussan to show the uniqueness of the solution of the Zakai equation.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In the framework treated in \cite{bensoussan , the measure valued solution of the Zakai equation, which is a stochastic partial differential equation was paired with the (function valued) solution of a deterministic backward PDE.\footnote{ The duality property is shown through the use of a collection of exponential martingales first introduced in the filtering framework by Krylov and Rozovskii.\cite{KR The proof in \cite{bensoussan involves the use of an It\^o's formula to deduce the evolution of the solution of a deterministic backward PDE integrated with respect to the measure valued process that is the solution of the Zakai equation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The same argument cannot be applied in the framework treated here since the coefficients of the operators appearing in the Zakai equation (hence also in the adjoint backward PDE) depend upon the current observation.
elements.sent-2411-041: As a result, the dual of the solution of the Zakai equation would now be the solution of a backward PDE starting at the time $t$ and run backwards in the interval $[0,t]$ and, at any time $s\in (0,t)$, would be a function of the observations on the interval $[s,t]$, making it random and, moreover, anticipating at any time $ s\in [0,t]$ the future of the observation process.
elements.sent-2411-042: As a result, the It\^o formula can no longer be applied.
elements.sent-2411-043: In order to circumvent this difficulty, we replace the dual deterministic PDE by a backward Stochastic Partial Differential Equation'' (or BSPDE for short).
elements.sent-2411-044: For this, we exploit recent results from Du, Meng \cite {DM and Du, Tang , Zhang \cite{DTZ which we need to adapt to our framework which requires a complex--valued BSPDE, equivalent to a system of two real valued BSPDEs, and construct Sobolev space valued solutions of our system of BSPDEs.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Moreover, we exploit classical Sobolev embedding theorems, in order to deduce enough smoothness of the solution of the BSPDE so that it can be integrated against an arbitrary measure--valued solution to the Zakai equation.
elements.sent-2411-046: The paper is structured as follows.

elements.sent-2411-049: Next we deduce the filtering equation (\ref{eq:filterEqns:ks ) for the conditional distribution of the signal, which is a non linear SPDE, as well as a linear equation (\ref{eq:filterEqns:zakaifunctint ) for the unnormalized version, see Theorem \ref {th:ex below.}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Finally we show in Theorem \ref{equivuniq that equation (\ref {eq:filterEqns:ks ) has a unique solution if and only if (\ref {eq:filterEqns:zakaifunctint ) has a unique solution.}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: In Section 4 we introduce several results pertaining to a class of backward stochastic partial differential equations that are used in the subsequent section as well as a useful It\^o type formula, see Theorem \ref{Zakaiextended and some preliminary Lemmas.}

elements.sent-2411-207: Following from Theorem \ref{th:ex , the process $\pi _{t}$ satisfies the evolution equation \begin{equation}d\pi _{t}(\varphi _{t})=\pi _{t}(\partial _{t}\varphi _{t}+A\varphi _{t})dt+\pi _{t}(B^{j}\varphi _{t})d\tilde{W}_{t}^{j}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-208: In the classical filtering problem (where $Y_t$ does not appear in the coefficients), one associates to the Zakai equation a proper adjoint backward PDE, which allows to establish uniqueness of a measure valued solution to the Zakai equation (this is Bensoussan's approach to showing uniqueness of the solution of the Zakai equation, see \cite{bensoussan for details).}
elements.sent-2411-209: However, in our situation, this approach is not feasible because the backward partial differential equation would involve the observation process $Y_t$ in its coefficients, resulting in a solution that is not adapted at each time $t$ to the past of that process.
elements.sent-2411-210: To address this issue, we employ an adjoint backward stochastic partial differential equation (SPDE) instead of an adjoint backward PDE.
elements.sent-2411-211: The solution to the SPDE remains adapted at each time $t$ to the past of the observation process.
elements.sent-2411-212: This approach will be developed in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-213: To facilitate this, we establish a new type of It\^o formula, which is essential for leveraging the duality between the Zakai equation and an adjoint BSPDE.
